title: Jamaican chicken soup
subtitle: Saturday soup with dumplings and ground provisions
excerpt: Bone-in chicken thighs simmered for an hour in a broth of scotch bonnet, allspice, thyme, and garlic, then loaded with cho cho, sweet potato, and small flour dumplings called spinners. The broth is Jamaica's answer to chicken noodle soup, thick, warming, and restorative. Saturday in Jamaica is soup day, and chicken soup is the most widely cooked version.
sourceNotes: Saturday soup is a Jamaican institution. Chicken soup with spinners (small flour dumplings) and ground provisions is the most common version, made throughout Jamaica and the diaspora every Saturday morning. The spinners are made from a simple flour-and-water dough rolled between palms into small cylinders. The practice of designating Saturday as soup day is documented in Jamaican cultural records from the 20th century.

content.text: Spinners are tiny, dense flour dumplings, not at all like gnocchi or European dumplings. They are made by rolling a small piece of firm dough between your palms into a thin cylinder about 3 cm long. They cook in the soup for 20 minutes and soften slightly, providing a starchy bite against the broth. The dough must be firm: too soft and they dissolve; too dry and they never soften properly.

Use bone-in, skin-on chicken thighs. The bones and skin give the broth depth that boneless chicken cannot. Remove the skin after cooking if you prefer a less fatty soup, the flavour has been extracted by then.

Mix the {{plain-flour}} with a pinch of {{sea-salt-fine}} and enough water (about 50 ml) to make a firm, non-sticky dough. Knead briefly for 1 minute. Pinch off small pieces (about 10 g each) and roll each between your palms into a thin cylinder 3 to 4 cm long. Set aside.

Place the {{chicken-thigh}} pieces in a large pot with 1.5 litres of cold water. Bring to a boil, skim the foam, and add the {{spring-onion}}, {{garlic}}, {{scotch-bonnet}} (whole), {{thyme}}, {{allspice}}, {{black-pepper}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Reduce to a steady simmer and cook for 30 minutes. Add the {{cho-cho}} and {{sweet-potato}} and continue simmering for 20 minutes. Add the spinners and cook for a further 20 minutes until they are cooked through. Taste and adjust the salt. Remove the scotch bonnet before serving. Serve in deep bowls.

items.fix: The spinner dough should be quite firm. If they dissolve, make the next batch firmer and roll them more tightly
The sweet potato starch and cho cho will naturally thicken the broth. Simmer uncovered for the last 20 minutes to reduce slightly
items.cause: Not enough vegetables or insufficient simmer time

items.fix: Add the scotch bonnet whole and remove at the 50-minute mark. A broken scotch bonnet releases much more heat
items.cause: Scotch bonnet broke or was over-infused

content.text: Chicken soup with spinners is Saturday soup in Jamaica. The tradition of a thick, slow-cooked soup every Saturday is rooted in Jamaican food culture. Every household has its version. The smell of chicken soup on a Saturday morning is a specific memory for anyone raised in Jamaica or in the Caribbean diaspora. In Britain the tradition continues in Jamaican families, made on Saturday morning and eaten at midday, a ritual that lasts across generations.
